title: Atrial Fibrillation: rate vs rhythm and anticoagulation
key_recommendations: In stable patients with new-onset AF, rate control (beta blocker or non-DHP calcium channel blocker) is reasonable first-line. Rhythm control reserved for symptomatic patients despite rate control, or younger patients with intolerable symptoms.
Anticoagulation decisions follow CHADS2-VASc; score >= 2 in men or >= 3 in women indicates anticoagulation.
Amiodarone is effective for rhythm control but has significant drug interactions and long-term toxicity. Consider DOAC or beta blocker alternatives first.
Echocardiography to assess LV function, valvular disease, and atrial size is indicated within 2 weeks of new AF diagnosis.
